perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall own, and Provider hereby irrevocably assigns to the Recipient, all rights, title, and interest in any invention, technique, process, device, discovery, improvement, or know-how, whether patentable or not and all other proprietary rights, industrial rights and any other similar rights, in each case on a worldwide basis, and all copies and tangible embodiments thereof, or any part thereof, in whatever form or medium hereafter made or conceived solely or jointly by Provider while working for or on behalf of the Recipient, which relate to, is suggested by, or results from the Services.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall own all rights, title, and interest in any invention, technique, process, device, discovery, improvement, or know-how, whether patentable or not and all other proprietary rights, industrial rights and any other similar rights conceived by Provider while working for the Recipient, unless Provider can demonstrate prior independent creation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that all inventions conceived by the Provider while working for the Recipient are owned by the Recipient. The modified text introduces an exception if the Provider can prove prior independent creation. This conflicts with the concept of 'work made for hire' doctrine under Israeli Copyright law, creating uncertainty about intellectual property ownership.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 addresses copyright ownership. The introduction of the 'unless Provider can demonstrate prior independent creation' clause deviates from the standard 'work made for hire' principle. It creates uncertainty and a higher burden of proof about who owns the intellectual property created as a result of the services performed under the contract, potentially violating copyright laws.

perturbation.justification: The original contract assigns all rights of inventions conceived by the Provider to the Recipient. The changed text introduces an exception where the Provider can demonstrate prior independent creation, potentially conflicting with intellectual property ownership laws. While the provided law snippet is generic regarding IP laws, the Perturbation and Law explanations highlight that this change deviates from the standard 'work made for hire' principle. The change introduces ambiguity regarding intellectual property ownership, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any term or provision of this Agreement that is held invalid or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction or other competent governmental authority in any situation in any jurisdiction shall not affect the validity or enforceability of the remaining terms and provisions hereof or the validity or enforceability of the offending term or provision in any other situation or in any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Any term or provision of this Agreement that is held invalid or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction in any situation in any jurisdiction shall automatically render the entire Agreement null and void.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies severability, meaning that if one part of the agreement is invalid, the rest remains valid. The modified text changes this to state that if any part is invalid, the whole agreement is voided. This contradicts the principle of severability which is recognized under contract law in Israel, and changes the risk allocation in the contract significantly.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19 (Partial Invalidity) of the Contracts Law (General Part) outlines the approach of Israeli courts to partially invalid contracts. It generally implies severability, unless it can be assumed that the contract would not have been entered into without the invalid clause. The modification removing severability directly contradicts this, making the entire agreement contingent on the validity of each individual clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 Error - Page Not Found Page Not Found 404 Error The page you requested could not be found. Try using the search box below or click on the homepage button to go there. Search button GO TO HOMEPAGE
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle of severability, which is generally implied under Israeli contract law as described in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the original clause specifies severability, and the modified text voids the entire agreement if any part is invalid, thus undermining severability. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized interpretations regarding severability, potentially making the contract unenforceable in its entirety due to a single invalid clause.
